title: When to Use FastText
concept: FastText is the default choice when dealing with user-generated text containing high levels of typos, slang, and novel compound words. Boss explain ends with: 'Next up: Recurrent Neural Networks.'

exercises.prompt: Which type of text data is hardest for a standard dictionary-based word model to understand?

options.text: Social media comments with typos and slang
Official legal contracts written by lawyers

exercises.explain: When users type freely, they make mistakes, use shortcuts, and invent words. Standard models fail here, which is why we need models that look inside words.

cards.title: The Internet is Messy
cards.body: On social media and chat apps, people don't type like Shakespeare. They write "soooooo hungry", make typos, and invent new words every day. If your AI only understands perfect dictionary words, it will fail. How do we help AI read the messy internet?

cards.title: What is FastText?
cards.body: FastText is a library created by Facebook that represents words as bags of smaller character sequences (called n-grams). This design allows it to understand misspelled words, slang, and brand-new vocabulary by looking at the familiar parts inside them.

cards.title: The Spelling Bee Champion
cards.body: Think of standard word embeddings like a dictionary of whole words. If a word isn't in that dictionary, the computer is completely lost. FastText is like a spelling bee champion who knows prefixes, suffixes, and roots. Even if they hear a brand-new word like 'un-dog-like-ness', they can break it down into parts and guess exactly what it means!

cards.title: Handling Creative Slang
cards.body: Imagine your app gets the input "superfastish". Standard systems see an unknown word and give up. FastText breaks it down into pieces like "super", "fast", and "ish". It instantly knows it has to do with high speed, even though it has never seen that exact word before!

cards.title: The Clean Data Myth
cards.body: You might think that to handle typos, you have to pre-clean your entire dataset and fix every spelling mistake manually. In reality, FastText handles these naturally because a typo like 'speling' still shares most of its character parts with the correct word 'spelling'!

cards.title: Whole Words vs. Sub-words
cards.body: See how different models process the slang word "hyperspeedy".

right.text: Breaks it into "hyper", "speed", and "y", understanding the meaning.

left.text: Sees "hyperspeedy" as a single unknown word and ignores it entirely.

exercises.prompt: What makes FastText uniquely suited for dealing with words it has never seen before?

options.text: It looks up the words in an official online dictionary.
It deletes any unrecognized words to save time.
It builds word meanings from smaller letter patterns.
It asks the user to retype the word.

exercises.explain: Because FastText breaks words down into sub-word pieces, it can guess the meaning of unseen words by looking at their familiar components.

exercises.prompt: Is FastText a poor choice for datasets that have a lot of user typos?
exercises.explain: FastText excels at slang and typos because it looks at sub-word parts rather than treating every word as an indivisible unit.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to complete the sentence:
exercises.sentence: FastText is the default choice when your text data contains many spelling _____ and slang words.
exercises.explain: FastText breaks words into smaller character chunks, making it highly effective at handling spelling mistakes and internet slang.

exercises.prompt: Which of these text snippets highlights the main reason you would choose FastText over standard word models?

options.text: Text A: "The economic forecast indicates moderate growth in the upcoming fiscal quarter."
Text B: "This new food delivery app is super-delishhh and uber-quick!"
Neither text shows a reason to prefer FastText.

exercises.explain: Text B shows a real-world scenario where FastText is ideal: handling informal social media posts containing typos ('delishhh') and composite slang ('uber-quick'). Text A is clean and standard, which doesn't showcase FastText's strengths.

exercises.prompt: You are building a chat filter for a mobile game where players constantly type slang, write typos like 'grreeat', and invent new words. Which tool should you choose to understand these words?

options.text: Standard Word2Vec because it ignores unrecognized words completely
FastText because it breaks words into character n-grams to handle typos
A simple vocabulary index that flags every typo as an error
A model that only works with perfectly edited books

exercises.explain: FastText is your go-to model when your text dataset is messy, full of internet slang, typos, or compound words, because it looks at word fragments instead of just whole words. Next up: Recurrent Neural Networks.
